speaker: interviewer
text: Gizella Kleinman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sometime I just, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your liberation--

speaker: interviewee
text: I get--

speaker: interviewer
text: --date again, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your liberation date, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: May the 5th.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was my [INAUDIBLE]. 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We were just talking about how this woman had three Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue to tell me that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One day, I don't know-- I see they're taking up food there-- pots, big pots of food. And I ask her, what-- who is there that you need to take up food there? She says, I'm going to tell you. I have three Russian soldiers there, hiding. They are-- they're [INAUDIBLE] in their uniform. They had the right boots. How do I know that? I didn't see them. Oh, yeah, I was-- I was, like, not very-- I mean, I was locked up. I didn't go around, like with people or something. But every Sunday, they took me to the church. That was my outgoing. And I didn't say no.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- did they know you were Jewish, at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I never told them that I'm Jewish. I never talk about it. My sister, [? Vesecra, ?] when I was with [? Vesecra, ?] nobody was there, we would talk in Jewish. But otherwise, I don't know. He never mentioned anything, I didn't mention anything, and that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took me to the church, and I had to cross, and I had to kneel-- you know, all those things. Because I felt that, that maybe-- maybe they know, and maybe they don't know. So I'm a Christian, so I have to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my sister didn't do it. Never. She actually never-- we-- I went into her and we were talking, you know, Czechoslovakian, but otherwise, they never took her out. Well, I had-- I didn't have hair, either, because they shaved me in [? Mauthausen ?]. But I had-- there they gave me a kerchief. We were full of lice. They clean our lice. They give us clothes. They were very nice. And they were watching us, what we were eating. They had fat food, they put it in a [? side ?] we should find it. They were very nice. And I used to watch over the kids she had. She had two little girls, and sometimes I used to play with them, and, you know, things like that. But my sister didn't have that. She was really, like, locked up there. It was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed about five weeks, I think. It was five weeks till we're liberated. But mind you, they didn't let us go home. The [INAUDIBLE] came home, we couldn't go anywhere. But since I was always thinking that my father was a [INAUDIBLE] man-- I knew my mother, where she is, that she [? never-- ?] and I was also thinking, maybe my older brother is home. And we wanted to go home. And we did. It took us awhile till we-- they actually didn't want that we should go right away, because they knew it's-- it's very hard. You know, everything is broken. But they took us with trucks till-- till Prague, I guess-- to Prague. But yeah, that Russian man-- one of them-- I mean, they came up, and they liberated, they had-- we had the speech. Anyway, so the-- one of the Russians-- they were not very young ones. They had family at home, you know. They were also probably hiding. That-- you know, that. And-- yeah, and he said like that, you come with me, you don't have to worry about [INAUDIBLE]. I'm a very family man. Don't worry about me going to the Germans. And we went there, and as we came in at one house, he had the rifle-- he was-- he was sitting like that, on the table. And they were begging they shouldn't do anything. And they took-- they were looking for all kind of things. I was looking for clothes. And I took two suitcases of clothes for me. One for me, one for my sister. And as we went away-- oh yeah. We came to Prague. We had till Prague. Prague, we came. We went on the train, the normal train. And we were tired, sitting down, and we had the suitcases-- like beside-- you couldn't put them up, the suitcases, like here, you go in the train. Anyway, they took away our suitcases. And we were so depressed, crying. And now we didn't have again-- didn't have clothes, you know. Just what we had there-- that skirt, or whatever-- a dress, that's what we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to from Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went home, to Munkatch.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was horrible. It was terrible. I came home, and I felt like my heart is staring off. It was horrible. And I came like-- I came in, there were soldiers there, sick people. We made a hospital from the house-- from that house. And there was soldiers beds. And so there was one woman. I spoke Russian at that time [INAUDIBLE]. So I told her, you know, this is my house. She said, yeah, it's your house, but now it's a hospital. If you want to stay, you could stay here. You could have food here, if you want. But there was a summer [? kitchen, ?] like. And she said she's going to put some beds there, do soldier beds, and we're going to sleep there, me and my sister. Anyway, I was three days there, I was afraid. I didn't sleep one night. And we went away from there. They didn't really-- at Munkatch, they didn't really-- did nothing for people who came home from the Holocaust. We didn't have where to go, we didn't have where to sleep. And I met my husband, already, in [INAUDIBLE] that coat. Somebody gave me an old coat. It didn't fit. It was very big. And-- and I was looking for a tailor to fix it. Didn't have a machine, didn't have nothing. I couldn't do anything. So I went in, and there I met my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Munkatch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And my husband remembers that-- it was across what kind of house we had there. I met a man, his daughter was with me in Bergen-Belsen, but she didn't survive. And he asked me-- he survived, the father survived-- I didn't tell him right away she didn't survive. But later on, he said he has a house there-- his own house, what he find, there was nothing there. He was sleeping on the floors. You can close the doors-- the knob was taken out. They took out everything from that house-- even the knobs, couldn't close the door. And we were sleeping there on the floor. And it was across the street, my husband was there, [? he saw-- ?] it was an awful thing. And then my sister got very sick. Still we came home, but she's better. But then she got very sick in that house. One night, she get convulsions. She got very sick, and I got very scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they opened up, before a little bit, he opened up a kitchen for people, but not for staying-- like, you know, to sleep or something-- but that kitchen, it was a kosher kitchen, but they open up for people who came from the Holocaust, that they should have what to eat. It was, you know, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, 3 o'clock in the morning, I was running in that kitchen there, they should send me a doctor. And they-- 6 o'clock in the morning, they send me a doctor. And I said, yeah-- and he said, listen, your sister is very sick. If you're not going to take her right now to the hospital, she has a very bad tuberculosis, and she'll die.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that second cousin, that I told you, he was also in Munkatch that time. He also was liberated, and he came to Munkatch. And he helped me and  my sister, to bring to Budapest. [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: To Budapest. He said, if you're going to put her in Munkatch, there, it's not going to be good. They're not going to do for her what she needs, and Budapest, there's a much bigger hospital there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took her there-- meanwhile I was, myself, sick. I had boils on my whole body. I had to go myself. When I took her to Budapest, I came back to Munkatch. I had to go into hospital because the boils were terrible. I had on my feet, and on my body-- everywhere. So they put me in a hospital. There was venereal diseases, things like that. And I see this one is talking there, that kind of a man. [INAUDIBLE]. I said to the doctor, doctor came in, I said, listen-- this is-- I'm not that kind of a girl, and I'm not going to stay. So every day, I had to go for packing-- they put whole packs on my feet, and medication. Anyway, I got better from that. Did you ever find any of your family, other than the second cousin back in Munkatch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nope, nobody-- nobody at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you reunited with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my brother? My brother find me in Germany. But this is something else, what I have to tell you. Anyway, my sister remained in Budapest, and I came back because I was sick also, and I came back. After that, I wanted to go back to Budapest to see my sister again. Meanwhile, they closed the border. All the trains would come in, they just went through. One Slovakian train stopped, and I went on it. And I came to Czechoslovakia-- to Slovakia, actually. It was a-- and I didn't see my sister. That's what's terrible. I didn't know what happened to her, because I couldn't get to her. The borders were closed, and-- Well, anyway, I went to Czechoslovakia, deeper in Czechoslovakia, and I was working there for awhile. And I had a house from the Germans. We got a house, what the Germans had to go out from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was later. The DP camp, I went later.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a house there, and with another friend. And I was working for little while in a factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sewing buttons and-- and pants with a machine. It's with a machine. But then I decided, they said that people was boarding in Munkatch, and that's Russian now. They're going to-- the're going to send them back. So I didn't want that. So I went further. We went to Asch. Somebody took us cross the border-- me, another girl, took us to the border. Not just me, a few people. You know, they took us. And we came in to Munich. They brought us to Munich. From Munich, I went to DP-- DP camp, back. Meanwhile, my sister came out with the children they sent out from Budapest-- the children. And she came to-- she came to Germany, also. But she came straight in a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Valstadt ?] Pocking. And I met my husband there, again. He came out from there, and we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel Kleinman. [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE]. And we got married, and my daughter was born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946. My daughter was born in 1947. It was awful [INAUDIBLE]. There was 5,000 people there in that camp. It was-- there's no privacy. It was awful. Got married, and with three couples living in one little place. So when I got married, they had to go out, somewhere else between other people. It was-- it was a horrible place. But from there, we-- we came to Canada. We were there a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about how you were reunited with your brother in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, he find me in Germany. He just find me from the blue, from the Red Cross, somehow. I don't know. He just came, and that's it. And he was there with me. He-- he became a policeman there. And we were together. And my sister, she was in that hospital. She used to come to me every weekend. They let her out for the weekends. So we were together. And well, then we decided that we want to go out somewhere. So they were looking for tailors.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my husband said he's a tailor, and we were waiting to go to Canada. And my brother, he went to Israel. He was fighting for the Haganah. It was very hard, at that time, to go to Israel. And he was in Israel there for awhile. For a long time, he was in Israel. And my sister, she got married in Germany, too. Her husband was also in the same-- in the same hospital that she was, and they met there. And they got married. They stayed in Germany for a long time, because nobody-- they couldn't come nowhere, because they had the tuberculosis. And they were-- I mean, they were OK already, you know, but as soon as they made an x-ray in the lungs-- you know? So they stayed for a long time. They made-- they made a little-- they opened a business there. And I guess they had-- they had some help from the Germans, also. So what--

speaker: interviewee
text: [COUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: --what year did you go to Canada? And why did you choose Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wanted-- Canada wanted tailors. That's why we chose [LAUGHS] Canada. We had to. We didn't have a choice. We just wanted to go out from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And ask me why Montreal? That we didn't choose, either. They brought us to Montreal. I wish they would have bring us to somewhere else. [COUGHS] What year did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I have a [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS WATER]

speaker: interviewer
text: We were just talking about your arrival into Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your children, starting with your child who was born in Germany. What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peggy, and Joe, and Ruth. Ruth came later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My children. My children are wonderful children, what can I tell you? Well, it was hard to bring them up, very hard. I had a very hard time. My husband very-- made very little money. And I lived in a room when I came to Canada, a very small one. And my husband, I don't know, made $15 or something like that a week. And it was $30, the rent. And sometimes, it was a time when we didn't have no food. But we didn't go nowhere. We didn't ask nobody, from nobody anything. What we had, I tried to share. I made a [? chicken, ?] I made [INAUDIBLE]. And then I have just Peggy. She was one and a half years when I came to Canada. But then, I got pregnant with my son later, two years--- two years later. And first I came, I was in a room. It was a horrible room. There was a woman, she-- she weighed about 300 pounds, very poor. Her husband was 70 years old, she was 34. That would be in a room like this. And I was crying, day and night. Little bugs everywhere. And my daughter was crying all night, because the bugs are biting her. And it was a horrible, horrible place. But then, I don't know, we couldn't stay anymore. We find another room, another place. Again, in another room. That time, I think, the second time, my husband made a little bit more money-- not too much, also. And I lived about for seven months there. And she find out that I'm pregnant, and she says, no way. There is no place for me. You have to go somewhere else. So what we did, as it was very hard to find a house, and they wanted us a lot of key money-- $700 key money. We find a house. It was a big house, old furniture. You touch them, they fall apart. But he wanted $700 key money. So we went to, a [INAUDIBLE], call it, and we borrowed money. So somebody signed for us, a friend who came a year before us. And we got money, and we moved it in the house, the big house. It was a very dirty house, but I didn't have money to paint.

speaker: interviewee
text: So but what I did, there was an upstairs and a downstairs, and the upstairs I rented it. I had a bunch of boarders there, three boarders there. And that's how I had my son. He had a place already downstairs. And I had boarders, they paid me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I also made food for some of them. So that's how I was better in a little bit. To bring up the children, it was a very, very-- we had a very hard time with-- you know, that's why we had two, and we didn't have more, because-- later on, my husband got a better job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have six grandchildren, I have one great-grandchild.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell me the names of your grandchildren, and your great-grandchild?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jonathan, Daniel, Stephanie-- that's my daughter, my son is Shannon, Ariel, and Jamie-- that's my daughter in Canada. And I have now a great-grandchild, Yael Devorah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who does she belong to? Who's her mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the granddaughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you, she is Stephanie. And her husband, [? Joshua. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Since the war, how has your faith, your religious faith, changed? Or has it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It didn't change. I kept kosher, right away. Matter of fact, when I came home, I was looking for kosher. And my friends tell me, are you crazy? You came home from such a terrible place, and now you're looking for kosher? I says, yes, I'm looking for kosher, because I'm brought up like that, and I'm not going to change it. My family-- I don't want to be different. And of course I'm not maybe Hasidic-- because my family was-- but we don't do nothing. We keep strictly kosher. And my husband is always in Shul, and I am in Shul. And we keep a nice Jewish family [INAUDIBLE]. I just couldn't throw away my religion. Sometimes you-- you know, sometimes you think about it. How does-- you know, rebel a little bit. Still how God could do that, think like that. Such a religious people, such a fine people, such-- you know, life goes on.

speaker: interviewer
text: If there was a message that you could tell your family, your children, your grandchildren, and great-grandchildren, or anybody who might be watching your testimony, what kind of message would you tell them about your experiences in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the message is that I-- if we would know before what was happening to us, maybe we would have done it different. But since we are here, and we survived, we are here to tell you what-- I hope it never happen, but it could happen. And I hope they're not going to let it happen. That's my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for your time today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
